title: A Converse Theorem for Hyperbolic Surface Spectra and the Conformal Bootstrap
speaker: Anshul Adve
abstract:
Simonyi 101
Given a compact hyperbolic surface of fixed topology, we consider its Laplace eigenvalues together with the structure constants for multiplication with respect to a suitable orthonormal basis of Laplace eigenforms. These numbers obey algebraic constraints analogous to the conformal bootstrap equations in physics. The main result of this talk is a converse theorem for these constraints: any collection of numbers satisfying the constraints must come from a hyperbolic surface. 
I will also briefly mention applications of these constraints to upper bounds for spectral gaps and subconvex bounds for L-functions. No knowledge of physics or L-functions will be assumed.

proof_stages.label: Spectral setup and inverse recognition problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Laplace spectra on compact manifolds, specializes compact hyperbolic surfaces as quotients of the hyperbolic plane with unit tangent bundle G/Gamma, and poses the problem of recognizing which sequences arise as genus-g hyperbolic surface spectra.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicative spectrum as enhanced data
proof_stages.summary: A compact hyperbolic surface is assigned an enhanced spectrum consisting of the Laplace spectrum, a holomorphic spectrum encoding the topology, and structure constants for pointwise multiplication in an orthonormal basis of L^2 of the unit tangent bundle.

proof_stages.label: Representation-theoretic adapted basis
proof_stages.summary: The Laplace and holomorphic spectra determine the irreducible PSL_2(R)-representations in L^2(G/Gamma), whose K-weight decompositions give automorphic forms, one-dimensional weight spaces, a GK-adapted basis, and an explicit topological indexing set.

proof_stages.label: Bootstrap equations and converse theorem
proof_stages.summary: Candidate spectra are defined and the main theorem asserts that such data come from a compact hyperbolic surface exactly when six hyperbolic bootstrap equations hold, encoding commutativity, weights, conjugation, the unit, the raising-operator product rule, and associativity.

proof_stages.label: Consequences of quadratic bootstrap identities
proof_stages.summary: After using the linear equations to eliminate variables, the associativity equation becomes a source of quadratic positivity identities that yield numerical spectral-gap bounds and high-energy bounds on structure constants, including arithmetic L-function applications.

proof_stages.label: Abstract reconstruction of representation and product
proof_stages.summary: For a candidate spectrum satisfying the equations, the proof begins by invoking Gelfand duality as a reconstruction tool, building the prescribed abstract unitary G-representation, and defining on it the multiplication map with the given structure constants.

proof_stages.label: Analytic control and Gelfand compact G-space
proof_stages.summary: Bootstrap-derived bounds on the structure constants make multiplication by each relevant vector a bounded operator, generating a commutative unital C*-algebra whose Gelfand spectrum X carries a continuous G-action.

proof_stages.label: Invariant measure and homogeneity recovery
proof_stages.summary: A canonical G-invariant probability measure identifies the abstract Hilbert representation with L^2(X,mu), and a Sobolev-type boundedness property for G-smooth vectors forces X to be homogeneous, hence X is G/Gamma with Gamma discrete cocompact.
